slides.title: What is a Ternary? ⚡
slides.content:
# The 3-Part Operator

Most operators you've learned are *binary* — they connect two things (like `5 + 3` or `score > 10`).

The **Ternary Operator** is special: it's the only operator in JavaScript that takes THREE pieces to work!

It is a massive shortcut for making quick decisions without writing long, multi-line blocks of code.

slides.title: The Syntax: ? and : 🎭
Ternary in Action 💻
slides.content: Try changing the points to be less than 50 and watch the message change instantly:

quizData.question: What will 'status' equal? let status = (10 > 20) ? "Big" : "Small";
quizData.explanation: Since 10 is NOT greater than 20, the condition is false. The ternary operator grabs the second value (after the colon), which is "Small".

slides.title: Use Responsibly! ⚠️
slides.content:
## Don't Chain Them

Ternary operators are amazing for simple, one-line decisions. But they can become an absolute nightmare to read if you stack them together.

```javascript
// DO NOT DO THIS!
let grade = (score > 90) ? "A" : (score > 80) ? "B" : (score > 70) ? "C" : "F";
```

**Golden Rule:** If your decision is complex or needs more than two outcomes, use standard `if/else` statements (which we will learn in Chapter 4!). Keep your ternaries short and sweet! 🍬
